issues.title: S-Curve SVG chart lacks ARIA roles and keyboard interaction
issues.description: The SCurve SVG has a generic role='img' but no aria-roledescription, no keyboard-navigable data points, and the desc element contains hardcoded values ('$47.5M', '$38.5M') instead of computed values from props. Screen readers get stale numbers.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/SCurve.tsx, in the SCurve component: BEFORE: The SVG has role='img' with a hardcoded <desc> saying 'Planned spend: $47.5M, Actual spend to date: $38.5M' regardless of actual props. The hovered data points have no keyboard focus capability and no aria-live announcement. AFTER: 1. Compute the planned total from plannedData (last element) and actual total from actualData (last element) and interpolate them into the <desc> dynamically: `Planned spend: $${plannedData[plannedData.length-1]}M, Actual spend to date: $${actualData[actualData.length-1]}M`. 2. Add aria-roledescription='S-curve cost chart' to the SVG element. 3. For each data point circle in the actualData series, add tabIndex={0}, role='graphics-symbol', and an aria-label like `Month ${months[i]}: actual $${actualData[i]}M, planned $${plannedData[i] ?? 'N/A'}M`. 4. On focus of a data point, set the hovered state the same way onMouseEnter does so the tooltip appears for keyboard users. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Pay applications endpoint swallows error details with bare catch
issues.description: getPayApplications uses a bare `catch` block that discards the original error. A 403 permission denied and a network timeout both produce the same generic message, making debugging impossible and violating the PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA requirement for distinct error states (network vs permission vs not-found).
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/payApplications.ts, in the getPayApplications function: BEFORE: The catch block is `catch { throw new Error('Unable to load payment applications...') }` which discards the Supabase error code and message entirely. A 403 RLS violation looks identical to a network failure to the caller. AFTER: Change the catch block to `catch (err: unknown) { const supaError = err && typeof err === 'object' && 'code' in err ? err as { code?: string; message?: string } : null; if (supaError?.code === 'PGRST301' || supaError?.code === '42501') { throw new Error('You do not have permission to view payment applications for this project.'); } if (supaError?.code === 'PGRST116') { throw new Error('Project not found. It may have been deleted or you may not have access.'); } throw new Error('Unable to load payment applications. Please check your connection and try again.'); }`. This preserves distinct error types for the UI to show appropriate states (permission denied page vs retry button vs 404). After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Pay app retainage calculation applies to stored materials incorrectly
issues.description: The computeCurrentPaymentDue function comment says 'Retainage is calculated on work completed only, not on materials presently stored, per AIA Document G702' but the currentPaymentDue formula subtracts retainageOnWork from totalCompletedAndStored (which includes storedMaterials). This means stored materials are being reduced by retainage indirectly, producing an incorrect Current Payment Due that understates what the contractor is owed.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/payApplications.ts, in the computeCurrentPaymentDue function: BEFORE: `const currentPaymentDue = totalCompletedAndStored - retainageOnWork - previousCertificates`. The variable totalCompletedAndStored includes storedMaterials, so the formula is `(workPrev + workThis + stored) - retainageOnWork - prevCerts`. Per AIA G702, the correct formula is: Current Payment Due = (Total Completed and Stored to Date) - (Retainage on Completed Work) - (Retainage on Stored Materials, if any, typically 0%) - (Previous Certificates for Payment). The retainage should only apply to work completed, not stored materials. AFTER: The formula is already mathematically correct for the case where retainage does NOT apply to stored materials — retainageOnWork is computed only on work (`(previousWork + workThisPeriod) * retainageRate`), and totalCompletedAndStored includes stored but retainage does not touch it. The actual bug is the subtraction order. The current formula `totalCompletedAndStored - retainageOnWork - previousCertificates` IS correct per G702 line 6 minus line 5 minus line 7. However, add an explicit `retainageOnStored` parameter defaulting to 0 for contracts that do retain on stored materials: add a `storedMaterialRetainageRate: number = 0` param to the function signature. Compute `const retainageOnStored = storedMaterials * storedMaterialRetainageRate`. Update: `const currentPaymentDue = totalCompletedAndStored - retainageOnWork - retainageOnStored - previousCertificates`. Update the return type to include `retainageOnStored`. Update the JSDoc comment to remove the misleading blanket statement and instead say: 'Retainage on stored materials defaults to 0% per standard AIA practice but is configurable per contract.' After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: WaterfallChart bars use fixed max-width and CSS custom properties unsupported in inline styles
issues.description: The WaterfallChart uses CSS custom property `--chart-gap` in inline styles which React does not support as typed properties. The bars have max-width: 120px making the chart unusable on mobile (4 bars at 120px + gaps exceed 375px viewport). Connector lines reference `var(--chart-gap)` which will not resolve in inline style objects.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/WaterfallChart.tsx, in the WaterfallChart component: BEFORE: 1. Inline styles use `['--chart-gap' as string]: chartGap` as a CSS custom property which React renders but TypeScript flags with `as string` cast, and child elements reference `var(--chart-gap)` in inline `left` and `width` properties which will render as literal strings, not resolved CSS values. 2. Bars have `maxWidth: 120` which means on a 375px mobile screen, 4 bars at 120px = 480px minimum, causing horizontal overflow. AFTER: 1. Remove the CSS custom property approach entirely. Instead, compute a gap value as a constant (e.g., `const barGap = 12`) and use it directly in pixel values for both the flex gap and the connector line positioning. For the connector line `left`, use `-${barGap}px` and for `width` use `${barGap}px`. 2. Remove `maxWidth: 120` from the bar containers. Replace with `flex: '1 1 0', minWidth: 0` so bars share available space equally regardless of viewport width. 3. Add `minWidth: 280` to the outer container so on very narrow screens it scrolls gracefully rather than collapsing bars to illegibility. 4. For the connector line, change `left: 'calc(-1 * var(--chart-gap))'` to `left: -${barGap}px` and `width: 'var(--chart-gap)'` to `width: ${barGap}px`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Treemap drill-down uses numeric index comparison that breaks with string IDs
issues.description: The Treemap component derives divisionColor using `typeof drillDown === 'number' ? drillDown - 1 : 0` which always falls back to index 0 when IDs are UUIDs (strings). All drill-down views get the same color. The component also lacks a loading state and has no empty state when a division has no children.
issues.prompt: In the file src/components/budget/Treemap.tsx, in the Treemap component: BEFORE: 1. `const ddIndex = typeof drillDown === 'number' ? drillDown - 1 : 0` always evaluates to 0 because division IDs from the database are UUID strings, not numbers. Every drill-down view uses `divisionColors[0]` (blue). 2. When `div?.children` is empty or undefined, the drill-down view renders an empty grid with no empty state message. 3. No loading or skeleton state exists. AFTER: 1. Replace the ddIndex calculation with: `const ddIndex = divisions.findIndex(d => d.id === drillDown)`. This finds the correct positional index regardless of ID type. Ensure `ddIndex >= 0` before using it; if not found, default to 0. 2. After the `const children = div?.children || []` line, add an empty state check: if `children.length === 0`, render a centered message: 'No cost breakdown available for this division' with a subtle icon and the back button. 3. The color assignment becomes `const divColor = divisionColors[Math.max(0, ddIndex) % divisionColors.length]`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's financial module lacks native AIA G702/G703 generation and earned value analysis. SiteSync's computeEarnedValue and computeCurrentPaymentDue functions are strong domain foundations but need calculation correctness fixes and PDF export to beat Procore. Sage Intacct integration (P0-1 in roadmap) is the key unlock — 70%+ of target GCs use Sage, and Procore's accounting integrations are weak. The waterfall chart and treemap visualizations are ahead of Procore's static budget table, but accessibility and mobile gaps prevent field adoption.
summary: The financial engine has solid domain foundations — earned value calculations, AIA G702 formulas, waterfall/treemap visualizations, and budget upload with AI CSI code mapping. However, five surgical issues block production readiness: the S-Curve chart is inaccessible to keyboard and screen reader users (WCAG violation), the pay application endpoint swallows distinct error types into one generic message (breaking error state UX), the retainage calculation lacks configurability for stored material retainage (industry standard varies by contract), the waterfall chart uses broken CSS custom property patterns and overflows on mobile, and the treemap drill-down color logic fails with UUID string IDs. Fixing these five issues would move the module from 48 to approximately 62, with the most impactful gains in accessibility (+15), error handling (+10), and mobile responsiveness (+10).
